name: Double-Time Feel
prompt: Switch the second drop into double-time by adding snares on every off-beat to halve the perceived bar.
definition: Double-time halves the perceived bar length by doubling rhythmic density.

name: Ghost Notes
prompt: Sprinkle quiet ghost snares at minus 24 dB between your main backbeats throughout the verse.
definition: A ghost note is a near-inaudible rhythmic note that fills space without taking focus.
wikiSummary: In music, notably in jazz, a ghost note is a musical note with a rhythmic value, but no discernible pitch when played. In musical notation, this is represented by a cross "×" for a note head instead of an oval, or parentheses around the note head. It should not be confused with the X-shaped notation that raises a note to a double sharp.

name: Rhythmic Displacement
prompt: Displace your main hook by an eighth note halfway through the second verse without changing any of its pitches.
definition: Rhythmic displacement shifts a pattern earlier or later than its expected downbeat.

name: Anacrusis (Pickup)
prompt: Lead every 8-bar phrase with a two-note pickup that lands on the downbeat of the next bar.
definition: An anacrusis is one or more notes before the downbeat that lead into the phrase.
wikiSummary: In poetic and musical meter, and by analogy in publishing, an anacrusis is a brief introduction. In music, it is also known as a pickup beat, or fractional pick-up, i.e. a note or sequence of notes, a motif, which precedes the first downbeat in a bar in a musical phrase.

name: Ostinato
prompt: Lock a 2-bar ostinato in the bass and never change a note of it for the entire track.
definition: An ostinato is a repeating rhythmic or melodic figure that anchors a groove.
wikiSummary: In music, an ostinato is a motif or phrase that persistently repeats in the same musical voice, frequently in the same pitch.

name: Dilla-Style Microtiming
prompt: Drag your snare 15 ticks behind the grid and push your hat 5 ticks ahead to get a drunken Dilla feel.
definition: Microtiming is the use of sub-grid offsets to define a feel like Dilla swing.

name: Groove Pocket
prompt: Nudge your bass to land 8 ticks late on every downbeat so it sits inside the kick pocket.
definition: Groove is the collective feel produced by intentional micro-deviations from the grid.
wikiSummary: In music, groove is the sense of an effect ("feel") of changing pattern in a propulsive rhythm or sense of "swing". In jazz, it can be felt as a quality of persistently repeated rhythmic units, created by the interaction of the music played by a band's rhythm section. Groove is a significant feature of popular music, and can be found in many genres, including salsa, rock, soul, funk, and fusion.

name: Isorhythm
prompt: Layer a 7-note pitch row over a 5-note rhythm row so the two cycles only realign every 35 hits.
definition: Isorhythm repeats a rhythmic pattern independently of a repeating pitch pattern.
wikiSummary: Isorhythm is a musical technique using a repeating rhythmic pattern, called a talea, in at least one voice part throughout a composition. Taleae are typically applied to one or more melodic patterns of pitches or colores, which may be of the same or a different length from the talea.

name: Euclidean Rhythm
prompt: Build your bassline using a Euclidean E(5,16) pattern and leave the algorithm to do the syncopation for you.
definition: A Euclidean rhythm distributes N hits across M steps as evenly as possible.
wikiSummary: The Euclidean rhythm in music was discovered by Godfried Toussaint in 2004 and is described in a 2005 paper "The Euclidean Algorithm Generates Traditional Musical Rhythms". The greatest common divisor of two numbers is used rhythmically giving the number of beats and silences, generating almost all of the most important world music rhythms, except some Indian talas. The beats in the resulting rhythms are as equidistant as possible; the same results can be obtained from the Bresenham algorithm.

name: Euclidean Percussion Layer
prompt: Stack three percussion layers as E(3,8), E(5,8) and E(7,16) and let them weave through each other.
definition: Stacked Euclidean rhythms create dense interlocking percussion textures.
wikiSummary: The Euclidean rhythm in music was discovered by Godfried Toussaint in 2004 and is described in a 2005 paper "The Euclidean Algorithm Generates Traditional Musical Rhythms". The greatest common divisor of two numbers is used rhythmically giving the number of beats and silences, generating almost all of the most important world music rhythms, except some Indian talas. The beats in the resulting rhythms are as equidistant as possible; the same results can be obtained from the Bresenham algorithm.

name: Additive Rhythm
prompt: Group your bar as 3+3+2 eighths and accent the first hit of each group throughout the track.
definition: Additive rhythm builds meter from grouped subdivisions like 3+3+2 instead of equal beats.
wikiSummary:
In music, the terms additive and divisive are used to distinguish two types of both rhythm and meter:A divisive rhythm is a rhythm in which a larger period of time is divided into smaller rhythmic units or, conversely, some integer unit is regularly multiplied into larger, equal units.
This can be contrasted with additive rhythm, in which larger periods of time are constructed by concatenating a series of units into larger units of unequal length, such as a 58 meter produced by the regular alternation of 28 and 38.

name: Metric Modulation
prompt: Redefine the pulse mid-track by treating your old triplet eighth as the new quarter note.
definition: Metric modulation redefines the pulse using a subdivision of the old tempo.
wikiSummary: In music, metric modulation is a change in pulse rate (tempo) and/or pulse grouping (subdivision) that is derived from a note value or grouping heard before the change. Examples of metric modulation may include changes in time signature across an unchanging tempo, but the concept applies more specifically to shifts from one time signature/tempo (metre) to another, wherein a note value from the first is made equivalent to a note value in the second, like a pivot or bridge. The term "modulation" invokes the analogous and more familiar term in analyses of tonal harmony, wherein a pitch or pitch interval serves as a bridge between two keys. In both terms, the pivoting value functions differently before and after the change, but sounds the same, and acts as an audible common element between them. Metric modulation was first described by Richard Franko Goldman while reviewing the Cello Sonata of Elliott Carter, who prefers to call it tempo modulation. Another synonymous term is proportional tempi.A technique in which a rhythmic pattern is superposed on another, heterometrically, and then supersedes it and becomes the basic metre. Usually, such time signatures are mutually prime, e.g., 44 and 38, and so have no common divisors. Thus the change of the basic metre decisively alters the numerical content of the beat, but the minimal denominator remains constant in duration.

name: Backbeat
prompt: Place a fat snare on every 2 and 4 and let the kick do whatever else it wants.
definition: A backbeat strongly accents beats 2 and 4 of a 4/4 bar.
wikiSummary: In music and music theory, the beat is the basic unit of time, the pulse, of the mensural level. The beat is often defined as the rhythm listeners would tap their toes to when listening to a piece of music, or the numbers a musician counts while performing, though in practice this may be technically incorrect. In popular use, beat can refer to a variety of related concepts, including pulse, tempo, meter, specific rhythms, and groove.

name: Off-Beat Open Hats
prompt: Put an open hi-hat on every 'and' of the bar and never let it fall on a downbeat.
definition: Off-beat hats fall on the 'and' between beats, a defining house sound.
wikiSummary: A hi-hat, hihat, or high-hat is a combination of two cymbals and a pedal, all mounted on a metal stand. It is a part of the standard drum kit used by drummers in many styles of music including rock, pop, jazz, and blues. Hi-hats consist of a matching pair of small to medium-sized cymbals mounted on a stand, with the two cymbals facing each other. The bottom cymbal is fixed and the top is mounted on a rod which moves the top cymbal toward the bottom one when the pedal is depressed.

name: Son Clave
prompt: Lock a 3-2 son clave into your rim shot and structure every other rhythmic layer around it.
definition: Son clave is a five-stroke rhythmic key in a 3-2 (or 2-3) pattern from Afro-Cuban music.
wikiSummary:
The clave is a rhythmic pattern used as a tool for temporal organization in Brazilian and Cuban music. In Spanish, clave literally means key, clef, code, or keystone. It is present in a variety of genres such as Abakuá music, rumba, conga, son, mambo, salsa, songo, timba and Afro-Cuban jazz. The five-stroke clave pattern represents the structural core of many Cuban rhythms. The study of rhythmic methodology, especially in the context of Afro-Cuban music, and how it influences the mood of a piece
is known as clave theory.

name: Tresillo
prompt: Use a 3-3-2 tresillo pattern for your kick and resist adding any additional downbeats.
definition: Tresillo is a 3-3-2 rhythm common across Afro-Latin and modern pop music.
wikiSummary: Tresillo is a rhythmic pattern used in Latin American music. It is a more basic form of the rhythmic figure known as the habanera.

name: Triplet Feel
prompt: Subdivide every beat in threes for the bridge so the whole section breathes in triplets.
definition: Triplet feel subdivides the pulse into groups of three throughout a section.

name: Stutter / Beat Repeat
prompt: Re-trigger your vocal slice at sixteenth then 32nd then 64th intervals in the last bar before the drop.
definition: A stutter or beat repeat re-triggers a slice at progressively smaller intervals.

name: Flam Ornament
prompt: Add a quiet flam grace note 30 ms before every backbeat snare for the whole verse.
definition: A flam is a drum rudiment with a quiet grace note just before the main hit.
wikiSummary: A drum rudiment is one of a number of relatively small patterns in drumming, a form of percussion music. Drum rudiments form the foundation for more extended and complex patterns.

name: Breakbeat
prompt: Replace your programmed drums with a chopped breakbeat sample and re-sequence its hits across the bar.
definition: A breakbeat is a sampled or programmed drum break used in place of four-on-the-floor.
wikiSummary: Breakbeat is a broad type of electronic music that uses drum breaks, often sampled from early recordings of funk, jazz, and R&B. Breakbeats have been used in styles such as Florida breaks, hip-hop, jungle, drum and bass, big beat, breakbeat hardcore, and UK garage styles.

name: Amen Break
prompt: Chop the Amen break at every transient and re-sequence those slices into a new 2-bar pattern.
definition: The Amen break is a six-second drum break that underpins much of jungle and dnb.
wikiSummary: The Amen break is a drum break that has been widely sampled in popular music. It comes from the 1969 track "Amen, Brother" by the American soul group the Winstons, released as the B-side of the 1969 single "Color Him Father". The drum break lasts seven seconds and was performed by Gregory Coleman.

name: Four on the Floor
prompt: Lock a kick on every quarter note at 124 BPM and refuse to drop a single one until the breakdown.
definition: Four on the floor places a kick drum on every quarter note of a 4/4 bar.
wikiSummary:
Four-on-the-floor is a rhythm used primarily in dance genres such as disco and electronic dance music. It is a steady, uniformly accented beat in 44 time in which the bass drum is hit on every beat .
This was popularized in the disco music of the 1970s
and the term four-on-the-floor was widely used in that era, since on a drum set, the beat is played with the pedal-operated, drum-kit bass drum.

name: Two-Step
prompt: Program a 2-step pattern with kicks on 1 and 3 and a swung snare on the off-beat between.
definition: Two-step puts kicks on 1 and 3 with a snare on the 'and' between, a UK garage staple.
wikiSummary: 2-step garage, or simply 2-step, is a genre of electronic music and a subgenre of UK garage. One of the primary characteristics of the 2-step sound – the term being coined to describe "a general rubric for all kinds of jittery, irregular rhythms that don't conform to garage's traditional four-on-the-floor pulse" – is that the rhythm lacks the kick drum pattern found in many other styles of electronic music with a regular four-on-the-floor beat.

name: Skank Stab
prompt: Put a short synth stab on every off-beat upstroke to import a ska skank into your track.
definition: A skank is an off-beat upstroke chord pattern from ska and reggae.

name: Accelerando
prompt: Ramp the tempo from 120 to 138 BPM over 32 bars without any other arrangement changes.
definition: Accelerando is a gradual increase in tempo over a passage.
wikiSummary: In musical terminology, tempo, measured in beats per minute, is the speed or pace of a given composition, and is often also an indication of the composition's character or atmosphere. In classical music, tempo is typically indicated with an instruction at the start of a piece and, if a specific metrical pace is desired, is usually measured in beats per minute. In modern classical compositions, a "metronome mark" in beats per minute, indicating only measured speed and not any form of expression, may supplement or replace the normal tempo marking, while in modern genres like electronic dance music, tempo will typically simply be stated in bpm.

name: Ritardando
prompt: Slow the tempo by 20 percent across the final 8 bars so the track winds down into stillness.
definition: Ritardando is a gradual decrease in tempo, usually toward an ending.
wikiSummary: In musical terminology, tempo, measured in beats per minute, is the speed or pace of a given composition, and is often also an indication of the composition's character or atmosphere. In classical music, tempo is typically indicated with an instruction at the start of a piece and, if a specific metrical pace is desired, is usually measured in beats per minute. In modern classical compositions, a "metronome mark" in beats per minute, indicating only measured speed and not any form of expression, may supplement or replace the normal tempo marking, while in modern genres like electronic dance music, tempo will typically simply be stated in bpm.

name: Rubato
prompt: Strip your breakdown of any grid and let one melodic line breathe entirely in rubato.
definition: Rubato is expressive elasticity of tempo for emotional phrasing.
wikiSummary: Tempo rubato is a musical term referring to expressive and rhythmic freedom by a slight speeding up and then slowing down of the tempo of a piece at the discretion of the soloist or the conductor. Rubato is an expressive shaping of music that is a part of phrasing.

name: UK Garage Shuffle
prompt: Run swung sixteenths at 65 percent on hats and snares while keeping the kick on 1 and 3 only.
definition: The UK garage shuffle layers swung sixteenth hats over a 2-step kick.
wikiSummary: UK garage, abbreviated as UKG, is a genre of electronic music which originated in England in the early to mid-1990s. It is defined by percussive, shuffled rhythms with syncopated hi-hats, cymbals, and snares, and may include either 4/4 house kick patterns or more irregular "2-step" rhythms. Garage tracks also commonly feature "chopped up" and time-stretched or pitch-shifted vocal samples complementing the underlying rhythmic structure at a tempo usually around 130 BPM. The genre was influenced by garage house, jungle, Jamaican soundsystem, ragga, dancehall, gospel music, R&B, and rave culture.

name: Amen Snare Displacement
prompt: Slide the Amen snare hits onto random 32nd-note offsets each bar so no two bars resolve the same way.
definition: Snare displacement reorders Amen break hits to break out of its native pattern.
wikiSummary: The Amen break is a drum break that has been widely sampled in popular music. It comes from the 1969 track "Amen, Brother" by the American soul group the Winstons, released as the B-side of the 1969 single "Color Him Father". The drum break lasts seven seconds and was performed by Gregory Coleman.

name: Trap Hi-Hat Rolls
prompt: Trigger a sixteenth-triplet hi-hat roll every fourth bar that ramps into 32nd notes by its end.
definition: Trap hi-hat rolls use triplet and faster subdivisions for movement over half-time drums.
wikiSummary: Trap music, also known simply as trap, is a subgenre of hip-hop music that originated in the Southern United States. Lyrical references to trap began appearing in 1991, while the modern sound of trap emerged in 1999. The genre takes its name from the Atlanta term "trap house", a drug house. Trap music features simple, rhythmic, and minimalistic production that uses synthesized drums. It is characterized by complex hi-hat drum beats, snare drums, and bass drums, some of which are tuned with a long decay to emit a bass frequency, originally from the Roland TR-808 drum machine.

name: Psytrance Rolling Bass
prompt: Roll a sixteenth-note bass between the kicks, hitting only on the e-and-a of each beat.
definition: Psytrance rolling bass plays sixteenths on the off-beats between four-on-the-floor kicks.
wikiSummary: Psychedelic trance, psytrance, or psy is a subgenre of trance music characterized by arrangements of rhythms and layered melodies created by high tempo riffs. The genre offers variety in terms of mood, tempo, and style. Some examples include full on, darkpsy, forest, minimal (Zenonesque), hitech psy, progressive, night-time, suomi, psy-chill, psycore, psybient, psybreaks, or "adapted" tracks from other music genres. Goa trance preceded psytrance; when digital media became more commonly used, psytrance evolved. Goa continues to develop alongside the other genres.

name: Trance Off-Beat Bass
prompt: Pluck a short root-note bass on every off-beat eighth between your four-on-the-floor kicks.
definition: Off-beat bass plays root notes on the 'and' of each beat between kick hits.
wikiSummary: Trance is a genre of electronic dance music that emerged from the techno and EBM scene in Frankfurt, Germany, in the late 1980s and early 1990s, and quickly spread throughout Europe.

name: Tihai
prompt: End your bridge with a phrase played three times in a row that lands back on the downbeat of the next section.
definition: A tihai is an Indian classical figure repeated three times to land on sam.
wikiSummary: Tihai is a polyrhythmic technique found in Indian classical music, and often used to conclude a piece. Tihais can be either sung or played on an instrument. Tihais are sometimes used to distort the listeners’ perception of time, only to reveal the consistent underlying cycle at the sam.

name: Tala Cycle
prompt: Loop a 16-beat tala cycle and mark the vibhags with subtle percussion changes instead of using bar markers.
definition: A tala is an Indian classical rhythmic cycle of fixed length divided into vibhags.
wikiSummary: A tala literally means a 'clap, tapping one's hand on one's arm, a musical measure'. It is the term used in Indian classical music similar to musical meter, that is any rhythmic beat or strike that measures musical time. The measure is typically established by hand clapping, waving, touching fingers on thigh or the other hand, verbally, striking of small cymbals, or a percussion instrument in the Indian subcontinental traditions. Along with raga which forms the fabric of a melodic structure, the tala forms the life cycle and thereby constitutes one of the two foundational elements of Indian music.

name: Konnakol Phrasing
prompt: Vocalize a polyrhythmic phrase in konnakol syllables and sample your own voice as the rhythm source.
definition: Konnakol is the South Indian art of vocalized rhythm syllables.
wikiSummary: Konnakol is the art of performing percussion syllables vocally in South Indian Carnatic music. Konnakol is derived from Telugu, it translates to “reciting the rhythmic syllables”. Konnakol is the spoken component of solkattu, which refers to a combination of konnakol syllables spoken while simultaneously counting the tala (meter) with the hand. It is comparable in some respects to bol in Hindustani music, as both allow for the composition, memorization, communication, and performance of rhythms. A similar concept in Hindustani classical music is called padhant.

name: Hocket
prompt: Distribute a single melody across two synths so each plays alternating notes without overlapping.
definition: Hocket distributes a single melodic line across alternating voices.
wikiSummary: In music, hocket is a rhythmic and linear technique involving the alternation of notes, pitches, or chords. In medieval practice, a single melody is shared between two voices such that one voice sounds while the other rests, creating a staggered, interlocking texture.

name: Reichian Phasing
prompt: Play identical patterns on two synths at 119.5 and 120 BPM and let them slowly drift out and back into phase.
definition: Phasing runs two identical patterns at slightly different tempos so they drift apart and realign.
wikiSummary: Phase music is a form of music that uses phasing as a primary compositional process. It is an approach to musical composition that is often associated with minimal music, as it shares similar characteristics, but some commentators prefer to treat phase music as a separate category. Phasing is a compositional technique in which the same part is played on two musical instruments, in steady but not identical tempo. Thus, the two instruments gradually shift out of unison, creating first a slight echo as one instrument plays a little behind the other, then a doubling effect with each note heard twice, then a complex ringing effect, and eventually coming back through doubling and echo into unison.

name: Risset Rhythm
prompt: Build a tempo illusion by layering rhythms at compounding doublings so the groove appears to accelerate forever.
definition: A Risset rhythm uses overlapping tempo layers to create the illusion of perpetual acceleration.
wikiSummary: Jean-Claude Raoul Olivier Risset was a French composer, best known for his pioneering contributions to computer music. He was a former student of André Jolivet and former co-worker of Max Mathews at Bell Labs.

name: Theme
prompt: Write a complete 16-bar theme that introduces every melodic idea your track will use later.
definition: A theme is a complete musical idea, longer and more developed than a motif.
wikiSummary: In music, a subject is the material, usually a recognizable melody, upon which part or all of a composition is based. In forms other than the fugue, this may be known as the theme.

name: 4-Bar Phrase
prompt: Confine every melodic idea to a strict 4-bar phrase with a clear beginning, middle and end.
definition: A phrase is a musical sentence with shape and direction, often four bars in EDM.
wikiSummary: In music theory, a phrase is a unit of musical meter that has a complete musical sense of its own, built from figures, motifs, and cells, and combining to form melodies, periods and larger sections.A phrase is a substantial musical thought, which ends with a musical punctuation called a cadence. Phrases are created in music through an interaction of melody, harmony, and rhythm.

name: Antecedent / Consequent
prompt: Write two paired 4-bar phrases where the first ends on the dominant and the second resolves to the tonic.
definition: A period pairs an antecedent phrase that opens with a consequent phrase that resolves.
wikiSummary: In music theory, a phrase is a unit of musical meter that has a complete musical sense of its own, built from figures, motifs, and cells, and combining to form melodies, periods and larger sections.A phrase is a substantial musical thought, which ends with a musical punctuation called a cadence. Phrases are created in music through an interaction of melody, harmony, and rhythm.

name: Real Sequence
prompt: Repeat your hook three times, each transposed up a minor third without adjusting any intervals to the key.
definition: A real sequence repeats a motif at a fixed interval without altering its intervals.
wikiSummary:
In music, a sequence is the restatement of a motif or longer melodic passage at a higher or lower pitch in the same voice. It is one of the most common and simple methods of elaborating a melody in eighteenth and nineteenth century classical music. Characteristics of sequences:Two segments, usually no more than three or four
Usually in only one direction: continually higher or lower
Segments continue by same interval distance

name: Tonal Sequence
prompt: Repeat your motif at three different scale degrees while keeping every note diatonic to the key.
definition: A tonal sequence transposes a motif within the key, adjusting intervals to stay diatonic.
wikiSummary:
In music, a sequence is the restatement of a motif or longer melodic passage at a higher or lower pitch in the same voice. It is one of the most common and simple methods of elaborating a melody in eighteenth and nineteenth century classical music. Characteristics of sequences:Two segments, usually no more than three or four
Usually in only one direction: continually higher or lower
Segments continue by same interval distance

name: Melodic Inversion
prompt: Take your main hook and mirror every interval around its first note, then use that as the bridge melody.
definition: Melodic inversion mirrors a motif's intervals around an axis pitch.
wikiSummary: In music theory, an inversion is a rearrangement of the top-to-bottom elements in an interval, a chord, a melody, or a group of contrapuntal lines of music. In each of these cases, "inversion" has a distinct but related meaning. The concept of inversion also plays an important role in musical set theory.

name: Retrograde
prompt: Reverse the note order of your main melody and use it as the lead in the second drop.
definition: Retrograde plays a melodic line in reverse, last note to first.
wikiSummary: A melodic line that is the reverse of a previously or simultaneously stated line is said to be its retrograde or cancrizans. An exact retrograde includes both the pitches and rhythms in reverse. An even more exact retrograde reverses the physical contour of the notes themselves, though this is possible only in electronic music. Some composers choose to subject just the pitches of a musical line to retrograde, or just the rhythms. In twelve-tone music, reversal of the pitch classes alone—regardless of the melodic contour created by their registral placement—is regarded as a retrograde.

name: Retrograde Inversion
prompt: Reverse and invert your hook simultaneously, then play it under the original as counterpoint.
definition: Retrograde inversion reverses a motif and mirrors its intervals.
wikiSummary: Retrograde inversion is the practice of flipping and reversing a musical passage. Used throughout music history, the technique gained prominence in the 20th century through the work of the Second Viennese School.

name: Augmentation
prompt: Restate your hook with every note doubled in duration as the foundation of your breakdown.
definition: Augmentation stretches each note of a motif to a longer duration, usually doubled.
wikiSummary: In Western music and music theory, augmentation is the lengthening of a note or the widening of an interval.

name: Diminution
prompt: Halve every note value of your hook for the second drop so the melody fires twice as fast.
definition: Diminution compresses each note of a motif to a shorter duration.
wikiSummary: In Western music and music theory, diminution has four distinct meanings. Diminution may be a form of embellishment in which a long note is divided into a series of shorter, usually melodic, values. Diminution may also be the compositional device where a melody, theme or motif is presented in shorter note-values than were previously used. Diminution is also the term for the proportional shortening of the value of individual note-shapes in mensural notation, either by coloration or by a sign of proportion. A minor or perfect interval that is narrowed by a chromatic semitone is a diminished interval, and the process may be referred to as diminution.

name: Motif Fragmentation
prompt: Take only the last 3 notes of your hook and develop them as their own phrase in the bridge.
definition: Fragmentation breaks a motif into smaller pieces that develop independently.
wikiSummary: In music, development is a process by which a musical idea is transformed and restated in the course of a composition. Certain central ideas are repeated in different contexts or in altered form so that the listener can consciously or unconsciously compare the various statements of the idea, often in surprising or ironic manners. This practice has its roots in counterpoint, where a theme or subject might create an impression of a pleasing or affective sort, but delight the mind further as its contrapuntal capabilities are gradually unveiled in a fugal development.

name: Liquidation
prompt: Gradually strip identifying features from your motif over 16 bars until only generic scale motion remains.
definition: Liquidation removes motivic characteristics until only neutral material remains.

name: Call and Response
prompt: Alternate a 2-bar statement on a lead synth with a contrasting 2-bar answer on a sampled voice.
definition: Call and response pairs a musical statement with a contrasting answer.
wikiSummary: In music, call and response is a compositional technique, often a succession of two distinct phrases that works like a conversation in music. One musician offers a phrase, and a second player answers with a direct commentary or response. The phrases can be vocal, instrumental, or both. Additionally, they can take form as commentary to a statement, an answer to a question, or a repetition of a phrase following or slightly overlapping the initial speaker(s). It corresponds to the call and response pattern in human communication and is found as a basic element of musical form, such as the verse-chorus form, in many traditions.

name: Leitmotif
prompt: Assign a 3-note motif to one specific sound and re-introduce it on a different patch every time that idea returns.
definition: A leitmotif is a recurring motif associated with a character, place or idea.
wikiSummary: A leitmotif or Leitmotiv is a "short, recurring musical phrase" associated with a particular person, place, or idea. It is closely related to the musical concepts of idée fixe or motto-theme. The spelling leitmotif is a partial anglicization of the German Leitmotiv, literally meaning "leading motif", or "guiding motif". A musical motif has been defined as a "short musical idea ... melodic, harmonic, or rhythmic, or all three", a salient recurring figure, musical fragment or succession of notes that has some special importance in or is characteristic of a composition: "the smallest structural unit possessing thematic identity".

name: Arch Contour
prompt: Shape your lead melody so it rises to a single peak in the middle and descends symmetrically to its origin.
definition: Melodic contour is the shape of a melody, like ascending, descending, arch or wave.
wikiSummary:
Melodic motion is the quality of movement of a melody, including nearness or farness of successive pitches or notes in a melody. This may be described as conjunct or disjunct, stepwise, skipwise or no movement, respectively. See also contrapuntal motion. In a conjunct melodic motion, the melodic phrase moves in a stepwise fashion; that is the subsequent notes move up or down a semitone or tone, but no greater. In a disjunct melodic motion, the melodic phrase leaps upwards or downwards; this movement is greater than a whole tone. 
In popular Western music, a melodic leap of disjunct motion is often present in the chorus of a song, to distinguish it from the verses and captivate the audience.

name: Stepwise Melody
prompt: Write a 16-bar melody using only stepwise motion with no leaps larger than a major second.
definition: Conjunct motion moves between adjacent scale degrees, mostly by step.
wikiSummary:
Melodic motion is the quality of movement of a melody, including nearness or farness of successive pitches or notes in a melody. This may be described as conjunct or disjunct, stepwise, skipwise or no movement, respectively. See also contrapuntal motion. In a conjunct melodic motion, the melodic phrase moves in a stepwise fashion; that is the subsequent notes move up or down a semitone or tone, but no greater. In a disjunct melodic motion, the melodic phrase leaps upwards or downwards; this movement is greater than a whole tone. 
In popular Western music, a melodic leap of disjunct motion is often present in the chorus of a song, to distinguish it from the verses and captivate the audience.

name: Leap-Only Melody
prompt: Write a melody where every move is a leap of a third or larger, with no stepwise motion at all.
definition: Disjunct motion moves by intervals of a third or larger.
wikiSummary:
Melodic motion is the quality of movement of a melody, including nearness or farness of successive pitches or notes in a melody. This may be described as conjunct or disjunct, stepwise, skipwise or no movement, respectively. See also contrapuntal motion. In a conjunct melodic motion, the melodic phrase moves in a stepwise fashion; that is the subsequent notes move up or down a semitone or tone, but no greater. In a disjunct melodic motion, the melodic phrase leaps upwards or downwards; this movement is greater than a whole tone. 
In popular Western music, a melodic leap of disjunct motion is often present in the chorus of a song, to distinguish it from the verses and captivate the audience.

name: Compound Melody
prompt: Write a single lead line that jumps between two registers to imply two independent melodic voices.
definition: A compound melody is a single line that implies two voices through register-jumping.
wikiSummary: In Schenkerian analysis, unfolding or compound melody is the implication of more than one melody or line by a single voice through skipping back and forth between the notes of the two melodies. In music cognition, the phenomenon is also known as melodic fission.

name: Major Pentatonic
prompt: Restrict your lead melody to the five notes of the major pentatonic and never play another pitch.
definition: The major pentatonic is a five-note scale with no semitones built on the major mode.
wikiSummary: A pentatonic scale is a musical scale with five notes per octave, in contrast to heptatonic scales, which have seven notes per octave.

name: Minor Pentatonic
prompt: Write your hook in the minor pentatonic and refuse to add the second or sixth scale degrees.
definition: The minor pentatonic is a five-note minor scale missing the second and sixth.
wikiSummary: A pentatonic scale is a musical scale with five notes per octave, in contrast to heptatonic scales, which have seven notes per octave.

name: Blues Scale
prompt: Use the flat-five blue note as the climax pitch of your lead and let it bend in by a quarter tone.
definition: The blues scale adds a flattened fifth blue note to the minor pentatonic.
wikiSummary: The term blues scale refers to several different scales with differing numbers of pitches and related characteristics. A blues scale is often formed by the addition of an out-of-key "blue note" to an existing scale, notably the flat fifth addition to the minor pentatonic scale or the addition of the minor third to a major pentatonic scale. However, the heptatonic blues scale can be considered a major scale with altered intervals.

name: Whole-Tone Scale
prompt: Build your breakdown lead entirely from the whole-tone scale so no note feels grounded.
definition: The whole-tone scale is built entirely of whole steps and has no leading tone.
wikiSummary: In music, a whole-tone scale is a scale in which each note is separated from its neighbors by the interval of a whole tone. In twelve-tone equal temperament, there are only two complementary whole-tone scales, both six-note or hexatonic scales. A single whole-tone scale can also be thought of as a "six-tone equal temperament".

name: Octatonic Scale
prompt: Run a lead arpeggio through the diminished octatonic scale's alternating half-whole pattern.
definition: The octatonic or diminished scale alternates whole and half steps across an octave.
wikiSummary: An octatonic scale is any eight-note musical scale. However, the term most often refers to the ancohemitonic symmetric scale composed of alternating whole and half steps, as shown in the accompanying image. In classical theory, this symmetrical scale is commonly called the octatonic scale, although there are a total of 43 enharmonically inequivalent, transpositionally inequivalent eight-note sets.

name: Harmonic Minor
prompt: Write a lead in harmonic minor and ride the augmented-second between the sixth and seventh degree every phrase.
definition: Harmonic minor raises the seventh degree of the natural minor scale.
wikiSummary: A minor scale is a sequence of musical notes in which the third scale degree is a minor third above the tonic. The notes A–B–C–D–E–F–G form a prototypical minor scale.

name: Melodic Minor
prompt: Ascend with raised sixth and seventh degrees and descend with the natural minor every time.
definition: Melodic minor raises the sixth and seventh ascending and lowers them descending.
wikiSummary: A minor scale is a sequence of musical notes in which the third scale degree is a minor third above the tonic. The notes A–B–C–D–E–F–G form a prototypical minor scale.

name: Phrygian Dominant
prompt: Set your lead in Phrygian dominant and emphasise the flat-second to major-third interval as your hook.
definition: Phrygian dominant is the fifth mode of harmonic minor with a flat second and major third.
wikiSummary: In music, the Phrygian dominant scale also known as Phrygian ♮3 or Double Harmonic Major ♭7 is the fifth mode of the harmonic minor scale, the fifth being the dominant. It is also called the harmonic dominant, altered Phrygian scale, dominant flat 2 flat 6, Hijaz, or Freygish scale. It resembles the Phrygian mode but with a major third, rather than a minor third.

name: Dorian Mode
prompt: Centre your modal vamp on Dorian and use the major sixth as the resolving note of every phrase.
definition: Dorian is a minor mode with a raised sixth, often jazzy and ambiguous.
wikiSummary: The Dorian mode or Doric mode can refer to three very different but interrelated subjects: one of the Ancient Greek harmoniai ; one of the medieval musical modes; or—most commonly—one of the modern modal diatonic scales, corresponding to the piano keyboard's white notes from D to D, or any transposition of itself.

name: Phrygian Mode
prompt: Write a brooding bassline in Phrygian and let the flat-second land on every downbeat.
definition: Phrygian is a minor mode with a lowered second, brooding and Spanish in feel.
wikiSummary: The Phrygian mode can refer to three different musical modes: the ancient Greek tonos or harmonia, sometimes called Phrygian, formed on a particular set of octave species or scales; the medieval Phrygian mode, and the modern conception of the Phrygian mode as a diatonic scale, based on the latter. In Hindustani Classical Music, it is equivalent to the Bhairavi thaat.

name: Lydian Mode
prompt: Stick to Lydian and let the raised fourth ring out on every long sustained note of your pad.
definition: Lydian is a major mode with a raised fourth, dreamlike and suspended in feel.
wikiSummary: The modern Lydian mode is a seven-tone musical scale formed from a rising pattern of pitches comprising three whole tones, a semitone, two more whole tones, and a final semitone.

name: Mixolydian Mode
prompt: Use Mixolydian for your modal vamp and let the flat-seven anchor every cadence.
definition: Mixolydian is a major mode with a lowered seventh, common to rock and house.
wikiSummary: Mixolydian mode may refer to one of three things: the name applied to one of the ancient Greek harmoniai or tonoi, based on a particular octave species or scale, one of the medieval church modes, or a modern musical mode or diatonic scale, related to the medieval mode. The Hypomixolydian mode of medieval music, by contrast, has no modern counterpart.

name: Aeolian Mode
prompt: Stay in pure natural minor for the entire track and never raise the seventh.
definition: Aeolian is the natural minor scale built on the sixth degree of the major scale.
wikiSummary:
The Aeolian mode is a musical mode or, in modern usage, a diatonic scale also called the natural minor scale. On the piano, using only the white keys, it is the scale that starts with A and continues to the next A only striking white keys. 
Its ascending interval form consists of a key note, whole step, half step, whole step, whole step, half step, whole step, whole step. That means that, in A aeolian, a scale would be played beginning in A, move up a whole step to B, move up a half step to C, then up a whole step to D, a whole step to E, a half step to F, a whole step to G, and a final whole step to a high A.

name: Locrian Mode
prompt: Write your lead in Locrian and resist treating the diminished fifth as a passing tone.
definition: Locrian is a minor mode with a lowered second and fifth, unstable and rare.
wikiSummary: The Locrian mode is the seventh mode of the diatonic scale. It is either a musical mode or simply a diatonic scale. On the keyboard it is the scale that starts with B and uses only the white keys from there on up to the next higher B. Its ascending form consists of the key note, then: Half step, whole step, whole step, half step, whole step, whole step, whole step.

name: Modal Mixture (Melody)
prompt: Pull a flat-sixth from the parallel minor into the climax of your major-key lead.
definition: Modal mixture borrows scale degrees from a parallel mode into a melody.
wikiSummary: A borrowed chord is a chord borrowed from the parallel key. Borrowed chords are typically used as "color chords", providing harmonic variety through contrasting scale forms, which are major scales and the three forms of minor scales. Chords may also be borrowed from other parallel modes besides the major and minor mode, for example D Dorian with D major. The mixing of the major and minor modes developed in the Baroque period.

name: Chromaticism
prompt: Use chromatic passing tones between every chord tone in your lead for at least 8 bars.
definition: Chromaticism uses pitches outside the prevailing key as colour and motion.
wikiSummary: Chromaticism is a compositional technique interspersing the primary diatonic pitches and chords with other pitches of the chromatic scale. In simple terms, within each octave, diatonic music uses only seven different notes, rather than the twelve available on a standard piano keyboard. Music is chromatic when it uses more than just these seven notes.

name: Pitch-Axis Theory
prompt: Hold a static tonic note while cycling your modal centre through Ionian, Dorian and Phrygian over 24 bars.
definition: Pitch-axis theory holds one tonic constant while modulating between parent scales.
wikiSummary: Pitch axis theory refers to a way of thinking about chord progressions and modes, that was heavily used and popularized by the guitarist Joe Satriani.

name: Single Climax Pitch
prompt: Save the highest note in your melody for one moment, late in the track, and never repeat it.
definition: The climax pitch is the single highest note of a melody, ideally appearing once.
wikiSummary: The climax or turning point of a narrative work is its point of highest tension and drama, or it is the time when the action starts during which the solution is given. The climax of a story is a literary element.

name: Low Tessitura
prompt: Confine your lead melody to the octave below middle C and never reach above it.
definition: Tessitura is the part of a melody's range where it lives most of the time.
wikiSummary: In music, tessitura is the most acceptable and comfortable vocal range for a given singer. It is the range in which a given type of voice presents its best-sounding timbre. This broad definition is often interpreted to refer specifically to the pitch range that most frequently occurs within a given part of a musical piece. Hence, in musical notation, tessitura is the ambitus, or a narrower part of it, in which that particular vocal part lies—whether high or low, etc.

name: Narrow Range
prompt: Restrict your entire melody to a perfect fifth and find the variety within that ambitus.
definition: Range or ambitus is the span between the lowest and highest melodic note.
wikiSummary: In medieval music, ambitus is a Latin term literally meaning enclos[ur]e, and in Medieval Latin means the "range" of a melodic line, most usually referring to the range of scale degrees attributed to a given mode, particularly in Gregorian chant. In Gregorian chant specifically, the ambitus is the range, or the distance between the highest and lowest note. Different chants vary widely in their ambitus. Even relatively florid chants like Alleluias may have a narrow ambitus. Earlier writers termed the modal ambitus "perfect" when it was a ninth or tenth, but from the late fifteenth century onward "perfect ambitus" usually meant one octave, and the ambitus was called "imperfect" when it was less, and "pluperfect" when it was more than an octave.

name: Klangfarbenmelodie
prompt: Pass each note of your hook to a different instrument so the melody is sounded by timbre changes alone.
definition: Klangfarbenmelodie distributes a melody across changing timbres.
wikiSummary: Klangfarbenmelodie is a musical concept that treats timbre as a melodic element. Arnold Schoenberg originated the idea. It has become synonymous with the technique of fragmenting a melodic line between different timbres.

name: Cantus Firmus
prompt: Set a slow-moving 8-note cantus firmus in the bass and improvise faster counterpoint above it.
definition: A cantus firmus is a pre-existing melody used as the structural scaffolding for new counterpoint.
wikiSummary: In music, a cantus firmus is a pre-existing melody forming the basis of a polyphonic composition.

name: Heterophony
prompt: Layer two slightly different versions of the same melody on different patches so they decorate each other.
definition: Heterophony lets multiple voices play variant versions of the same melody simultaneously.
wikiSummary: In music, heterophony is a type of texture characterized by the simultaneous variation of a single melodic line. Such a texture can be regarded as a kind of complex monophony in which there is only one basic melody, but realized at the same time in multiple voices, each of which plays the melody differently, either in a different rhythm or tempo, or with various embellishments and elaborations. The term was initially introduced into systematic musicology to denote a subcategory of polyphonic music, though is now regarded as a textural category in its own right.

name: Alberti Bass
prompt: Replace your bassline with an Alberti pattern: root, fifth, third, fifth on repeating eighths.
definition: Alberti bass is a broken-chord accompaniment in a root-fifth-third-fifth pattern.
wikiSummary: Alberti bass is a particular kind of accompaniment figure in music, often used in the Classical era, and sometimes the Romantic era. It was named after Domenico Alberti (1710–1740/46), who used it extensively, although he was not the first to use it.

name: Walking Bass
prompt: Write a quarter-note walking bass that connects every chord change by step or chromatic approach.
definition: A walking bass plays a quarter-note line moving mostly stepwise between chord tones.
wikiSummary: Bassline is the term used in many styles of music, such as blues, jazz, funk, dub and electronic, traditional, and classical music, for the low-pitched instrumental part or line played by a rhythm section instrument such as the electric bass, double bass, cello, tuba or keyboard.

name: Tritone Paradox
prompt: Build a melody from ambiguous tritone leaps so listeners disagree on whether the line is rising or falling.
definition: The tritone paradox is an auditory illusion where listeners disagree on the direction of a tritone pair.
wikiSummary: The tritone paradox is an auditory illusion in which a sequentially played pair of Shepard tones separated by an interval of a tritone, or half octave, is heard as ascending by some people and as descending by others. Different populations tend to favor one of a limited set of different spots around the chromatic circle as central to the set of "higher" tones. Roger Shepard in 1963 had argued that such tone pairs would be heard ambiguously as either ascending or descending. However, psychology of music researcher Diana Deutsch in 1986 discovered that when the judgments of individual listeners were considered separately, their judgments depended on the positions of the tones along the chromatic circle. For example, one listener would hear the tone pair C–F♯ as ascending and the tone pair G–C♯ as descending. Yet another listener would hear the tone pair C–F♯ as descending and the tone pair G–C♯ as ascending. Furthermore, the way these tone pairs were perceived varied depending on the listener's language or dialect.

name: Root-Fifth Bass
prompt: Limit your bassline to roots and fifths only and pulse it in straight eighths under the chord changes.
definition: A root-fifth bassline alternates between the root and fifth of each chord.
wikiSummary: Bass ( BAYSS) (also called bottom end) describes tones of low (also called "deep") frequency, pitch and range from 16 to 250 Hz (C0 to middle C4) and bass instruments that produce tones in the low-pitched range C2-C4. They belong to different families of instruments and can cover a wide range of musical roles. Since producing low pitches usually requires a long air column or string, and for stringed instruments, a large hollow body, the string and wind bass instruments are usually the largest instruments in their families or instrument classes.

name: Pedal-Note Melody
prompt: Return to the same pedal note between every other melodic note in your hook for the first 8 bars.
definition: A pedal-note melody anchors a melodic line by repeatedly returning to one fixed pitch.
wikiSummary: In music, a pedal point is a sustained tone, typically in the bass, during which at least one foreign harmony is sounded in the other parts. A pedal point sometimes functions as a "non-chord tone", placing it in the categories alongside suspensions, retardations, and passing tones. However, the pedal point is unique among non-chord tones, "in that it begins on a consonance, sustains through another chord as a dissonance until the harmony", not the non-chord tone, "resolves back to a consonance".

name: Synthwave Octave Arp
prompt: Arpeggiate root, fifth, root one octave up, fifth on straight eighths under your entire chorus.
definition: A synthwave octave arp pulses root-fifth-root-fifth across two octaves.
wikiSummary: Synthwave is an electronic music microgenre that is based predominantly on the music associated with the soundtracks of action, science fiction, and horror films of the 1970s and 1980s. Other influences are drawn from the decade's art and video games. Synthwave musicians often espouse nostalgia for 1980s culture and attempt to capture the era's atmosphere and celebrate it.

name: Authentic Cadence (V-I)
prompt: Resolve every 8-bar phrase with a clean V to I cadence and never hide it behind passing chords.
definition: An authentic cadence moves from the dominant chord (V) to the tonic (I) for strong resolution.
wikiSummary: In Western musical theory, a cadence is the end of a phrase in which the melody or harmony creates a sense of full or partial resolution, especially in music of the 16th century onwards. A harmonic cadence is a progression of two or more chords that concludes a phrase, section, or piece of music. A rhythmic cadence is a characteristic rhythmic pattern that indicates the end of a phrase. A cadence can be labeled "weak" or "strong" depending on the impression of finality it gives.

name: Plagal Cadence (IV-I)
prompt: End your breakdown with a soft IV to I plagal cadence rather than a dominant resolution.
definition: A plagal cadence moves from the subdominant (IV) to the tonic (I), the gentler 'amen' cadence.
wikiSummary: In Western musical theory, a cadence is the end of a phrase in which the melody or harmony creates a sense of full or partial resolution, especially in music of the 16th century onwards. A harmonic cadence is a progression of two or more chords that concludes a phrase, section, or piece of music. A rhythmic cadence is a characteristic rhythmic pattern that indicates the end of a phrase. A cadence can be labeled "weak" or "strong" depending on the impression of finality it gives.

name: Half Cadence
prompt: Park every phrase on the dominant chord and resist resolving to the tonic until the final bar.
definition: A half cadence ends on the dominant chord, leaving the listener expecting resolution.
wikiSummary: In Western musical theory, a cadence is the end of a phrase in which the melody or harmony creates a sense of full or partial resolution, especially in music of the 16th century onwards. A harmonic cadence is a progression of two or more chords that concludes a phrase, section, or piece of music. A rhythmic cadence is a characteristic rhythmic pattern that indicates the end of a phrase. A cadence can be labeled "weak" or "strong" depending on the impression of finality it gives.

name: Deceptive Cadence (V-vi)
prompt: Set up a V chord with full build energy and resolve to vi instead of I for a fake drop.
definition: A deceptive cadence sets up a V to I resolution but swerves to vi instead.
wikiSummary: In Western musical theory, a cadence is the end of a phrase in which the melody or harmony creates a sense of full or partial resolution, especially in music of the 16th century onwards. A harmonic cadence is a progression of two or more chords that concludes a phrase, section, or piece of music. A rhythmic cadence is a characteristic rhythmic pattern that indicates the end of a phrase. A cadence can be labeled "weak" or "strong" depending on the impression of finality it gives.

name: Phrygian Half Cadence
prompt: Cadence on iv6 to V in a minor key to give your bridge an ancient, modal feel.
definition: A Phrygian half cadence moves from iv6 to V in a minor key.
wikiSummary: In Western musical theory, a cadence is the end of a phrase in which the melody or harmony creates a sense of full or partial resolution, especially in music of the 16th century onwards. A harmonic cadence is a progression of two or more chords that concludes a phrase, section, or piece of music. A rhythmic cadence is a characteristic rhythmic pattern that indicates the end of a phrase. A cadence can be labeled "weak" or "strong" depending on the impression of finality it gives.

name: Picardy Third
prompt: End your minor-key track on a sudden major tonic chord and hold it as the final reverb tail.
definition: A Picardy third ends a minor-key piece on a major tonic chord.
wikiSummary: A Picardy third, also known as a Picardy cadence or Tierce de Picardie, is a major chord of the tonic at the end of a musical section that is either modal or in a minor key. This is achieved by raising the third of the expected minor triad by a semitone to create a major triad, as a form of resolution.

name: Borrowed Chord
prompt: Drop a borrowed flat-VI from the parallel minor into the second half of your chorus.
definition: A borrowed chord is taken from the parallel mode of the current key.
wikiSummary: A borrowed chord is a chord borrowed from the parallel key. Borrowed chords are typically used as "color chords", providing harmonic variety through contrasting scale forms, which are major scales and the three forms of minor scales. Chords may also be borrowed from other parallel modes besides the major and minor mode, for example D Dorian with D major. The mixing of the major and minor modes developed in the Baroque period.

name: Secondary Dominant
prompt: Insert a V of V chord before your dominant to launch the build with extra harmonic momentum.
definition: A secondary dominant is the dominant chord of a non-tonic target.
wikiSummary: Secondary chords are a type of altered or borrowed chord, chords that are not part of the music piece's key. They are the most common type of altered chord in tonal music. Secondary chords are referred to by the function they have and the key or chord in which they function. In Roman numeral analysis, they are written with the notation "function/key". Thus, one of the most common secondary chords, the dominant of the dominant, is written "V/V" and read as "five of five" or "the dominant of the dominant".

name: Secondary Leading-Tone Chord
prompt: Use a vii diminished of ii to tonicize the second chord of your progression.
definition: A secondary leading-tone chord targets a non-tonic chord with its own leading tone.
wikiSummary: Secondary chords are a type of altered or borrowed chord, chords that are not part of the music piece's key. They are the most common type of altered chord in tonal music. Secondary chords are referred to by the function they have and the key or chord in which they function. In Roman numeral analysis, they are written with the notation "function/key". Thus, one of the most common secondary chords, the dominant of the dominant, is written "V/V" and read as "five of five" or "the dominant of the dominant".

name: Tritone Substitution
prompt: Replace your V7 with the flat-II7 a tritone away to slide chromatically into the tonic.
definition: Tritone substitution replaces a dominant seventh with another dominant seventh a tritone away.
wikiSummary: The tritone substitution is a common chord substitution found in both jazz and classical music. Where jazz is concerned, it was the precursor to more complex substitution patterns like Coltrane changes. Tritone substitutions are sometimes used in improvisation—often to create tension during a solo. Though examples of the tritone substitution, known in the classical world as an augmented sixth chord, can be found extensively in classical music since the Renaissance period, they were not heard outside of classical music until they were brought into jazz by musicians such as Dizzy Gillespie and Charlie Parker in the 1940s, as well as Duke Ellington, Art Tatum, Coleman Hawkins, Roy Eldridge and Benny Goodman.

name: Neapolitan Chord
prompt: Set up your dominant with a Neapolitan (flat-II major) in first inversion as the bridge's pivot chord.
definition: A Neapolitan chord is a major chord built on the lowered second degree.
wikiSummary: In Classical music theory, a Neapolitan chord is a major chord built on the lowered (flat) second (supertonic) scale degree. In Schenkerian analysis, it is known as a Phrygian II, since in minor scales the chord is built on the notes of the corresponding Phrygian mode. The Neapolitan is found far more often in minor keys than in major keys.

name: Augmented Sixth Chord
prompt: Lead into your dominant with a German augmented sixth chord for a dramatic chromatic pre-dominant.
definition: Augmented sixth chords are chromatic pre-dominant chords (Italian, French, German variants).
wikiSummary: In music theory, an augmented sixth chord contains the interval of an augmented sixth, usually above its bass tone. This chord has its origins in the Renaissance, was further developed in the Baroque, and became a distinctive part of the musical style of the Classical and Romantic periods.

name: Chromatic Mediant
prompt: Move from I to a chromatic-mediant III major as a cinematic chord change in your breakdown.
definition: A chromatic mediant is a chord a third away from the tonic with chromatic alterations.
wikiSummary: In music, chromatic mediants are "altered mediant and submediant chords." A chromatic mediant relationship defined conservatively is a relationship between two sections and/or chords whose roots are related by a major third or minor third, and contain one common tone. For example, in the key of C major the diatonic mediant and submediant are E minor and A minor respectively. Their parallel majors are E major and A major. The mediants of the parallel minor of C major are E♭ major and A♭ major. Thus, by this conservative definition, C major has four chromatic mediants: E major, A major, E♭ major, and A♭ major.

name: Negative Harmony
prompt: Reharmonise your chorus by reflecting every chord around the tonic-dominant axis.
definition: Negative harmony mirrors chords around a fixed tonic-dominant axis.
wikiSummary: In music theory, negative harmony is a concept that every musical note, and therefore every chord, has a corresponding “negative” counterpart produced by reflecting the pitch across an axis positioned on the middle point between the tonic and the dominant. The idea stems from the theory of polarity of Ernst Levy (1895–1981), presented in his book A Theory of Harmony. The term "negative harmony" was later developed and coined by American saxophonist Steve Coleman and gained broader attention in the early 21st century through the influence of English musician Jacob Collier.

name: Pedal Point
prompt: Sustain a low tonic pedal across all 16 bars of your build while the chords above it change freely.
definition: A pedal point is a sustained pitch held under shifting harmony above.
wikiSummary: In music, a pedal point is a sustained tone, typically in the bass, during which at least one foreign harmony is sounded in the other parts. A pedal point sometimes functions as a "non-chord tone", placing it in the categories alongside suspensions, retardations, and passing tones. However, the pedal point is unique among non-chord tones, "in that it begins on a consonance, sustains through another chord as a dissonance until the harmony", not the non-chord tone, "resolves back to a consonance".

name: Drone
prompt: Lay a fundamental-key drone under the entire track and never let it stop sounding.
definition: A drone is a continuous sustained low pitch used as a harmonic foundation.
wikiSummary: In music, a drone is a harmonic or monophonic effect or accompaniment where a note or chord is continuously sounded throughout most or all of a piece. A drone may also be any part of a musical instrument used to produce this effect; an archaic term for this is burden such as a "drone [pipe] of a bagpipe", the pedal point in an organ, or the lowest course of a lute. Α burden is also part of a song that is repeated at the end of each stanza, such as the chorus or refrain.

name: Modal Vamp
prompt: Oscillate between two chords for the whole track and never cadence to a tonic.
definition: A modal vamp is a two-chord oscillation that establishes a mode without cadencing.
wikiSummary: In music, an ostinato is a motif or phrase that persistently repeats in the same musical voice, frequently in the same pitch.

name: Loop-Based Harmony
prompt: Commit to a single 4-bar chord loop as the entire harmonic plan and rearrange around it.
definition: Loop-based harmony treats a short chord cycle as the complete harmonic structure.
wikiSummary: In a musical composition, a chord progression or harmonic progression is a succession of chords. Chord progressions are the foundation of harmony in Western musical tradition from the common practice era of classical music to the 21st century. Chord progressions are the foundation of popular music styles, traditional music, as well as genres such as blues and jazz. In these genres, chord progressions are the defining feature on which melody and rhythm are built.

name: Power Chord
prompt: Use only root and fifth power chords in your bass synth so the harmony stays mode-agnostic.
definition: A power chord uses only the root and fifth, omitting the third.
wikiSummary: A power chord, also called a fifth chord, is a colloquial name for a chord on guitar, especially on electric guitar, that consists of the root note and the fifth, as well as possibly octaves of those notes. Power chords are commonly played with an amp with intentionally added distortion or overdrive effects. Power chords are a key element of many styles of rock, especially heavy metal and punk rock.

name: Quartal Harmony
prompt: Voice every chord in stacked perfect fourths instead of tertian thirds.
definition: Quartal harmony builds chords by stacking fourths instead of thirds.
wikiSummary: In music, quartal harmony is the building of harmonic structures from the intervals of the perfect fourth, the augmented fourth, and the diminished fourth. For instance, a three-note quartal chord on C can be built by stacking perfect fourths, C–F–B♭.

name: Suspended Chord
prompt: Replace every major triad with a sus2 voicing and never resolve to the third.
definition: A suspended chord replaces the third with a second or fourth.
wikiSummary: A suspended chord is a musical chord in which the third is replaced by a dissonant tone like a perfect fourth or a major second. The resulting sound is tonally ambiguous. The practice is widespread in popular music.

name: Add9 Voicing
prompt: Voice every chord as an add9 with the ninth at the top of the voicing.
definition: An add9 chord adds the ninth scale degree to a triad without the seventh.
wikiSummary: An added tone chord, or added note chord, is a non-tertian chord composed of a triad and one or more extra "added" notes. Any tone that is not a seventh is commonly categorized as an added tone. It can be outside the tertian sequence of ascending thirds from the root, such as the added sixth or fourth, or it can be in a chord that does not consist of a continuous stack of thirds, such as the added thirteenth. The concept of added tones is convenient in that all notes may be related to familiar chords.

name: Seventh Chord
prompt: Replace every triad in your progression with its diatonic seventh chord.
definition: A seventh chord adds the seventh scale degree above a triad.
wikiSummary: In music, extended chords are tertian chords with notes beyond the seventh. Chords such as the Ninth, eleventh, and thirteenth chords are extended chords. The thirteenth is the furthest extension diatonically possible as, by that point, all seven tonal degrees are represented within the chord. In practice, however, extended chords do not typically use all the chord members: the fifth is often omitted, as well as the ninth and eleventh, unless they are altered to give a special texture.

name: Extended Chord
prompt: Voice your pad in 9, 11 and 13 extensions and avoid playing the root.
definition: Extended chords stack tertian extensions beyond the seventh.
wikiSummary: In music, extended chords are tertian chords with notes beyond the seventh. Chords such as the Ninth, eleventh, and thirteenth chords are extended chords. The thirteenth is the furthest extension diatonically possible as, by that point, all seven tonal degrees are represented within the chord. In practice, however, extended chords do not typically use all the chord members: the fifth is often omitted, as well as the ninth and eleventh, unless they are altered to give a special texture.

name: Slash Chord
prompt: Voice each chord with a non-root in the bass to walk a smooth bassline under static chord shapes.
definition: A slash chord places a non-root tone in the bass voice.
wikiSummary:
In music, especially modern popular music, a slash chord or slashed chord, also compound chord, is a chord whose bass note or inversion is indicated by the addition of a slash and the letter of the bass note after the root note letter. It does not indicate "or".
For example, a C major chord (C) in second inversion is written C/G or C/G bass, which reads "C slash G", "C over G" or "C over a G bass". Some chords may not otherwise be notated, such as A♭/A. Thus, a slash chord may also indicate the chord form or shape and an additional bass note.

name: Parallel Motion
prompt: Move every voice in your pad in parallel fifths or octaves so the chord shape never changes.
definition: Parallel motion moves voices in the same direction at the same interval.
wikiSummary: In music, parallel harmony, also known as harmonic parallelism, harmonic planing or parallel voice leading, is the parallel movement of two or more melodies.

name: Contrary Motion
prompt: Write your bassline descending while your top melody ascends, both diatonic, for 8 bars.
definition: Contrary motion moves voices in opposite directions, the strongest voice-leading.
wikiSummary: In music theory, contrapuntal motion is the general movement of two or more melodic lines with respect to each other. In traditional four-part harmony, it is important that lines maintain their independence, an effect which can be achieved by the judicious use of the four types of contrapuntal motion: parallel motion, similar motion, contrary motion, and oblique motion.

name: Oblique Motion
prompt: Hold a single voice static while every other voice in your pad moves around it.
definition: Oblique motion holds one voice still while others move.
wikiSummary: In music theory, contrapuntal motion is the general movement of two or more melodic lines with respect to each other. In traditional four-part harmony, it is important that lines maintain their independence, an effect which can be achieved by the judicious use of the four types of contrapuntal motion: parallel motion, similar motion, contrary motion, and oblique motion.

name: Smooth Voice Leading
prompt: Re-voice your progression so every chord change moves at most a major second in each voice.
definition: Voice leading is the art of moving individual voices between chords smoothly.
wikiSummary: Voice leading is the linear progression of individual melodic lines and their interaction with one another to create harmonies, typically in accordance with the principles of common-practice harmony and counterpoint. These principles include voices sounding smooth and independent, generally minimising movement to common tones as well as steps to the closest chord tone possible, therefore minimising leaps where possible. As a result, different voicings and inversions of chords may provide smoother voice leading.

name: Common-Tone Modulation
prompt: Modulate to a distant key by holding one shared pitch as the pivot between the two chords.
definition: Common-tone modulation pivots between keys through a shared pitch.
wikiSummary: In music, modulation is the change from one tonality to another. This may or may not be accompanied by a change in key signature. Modulations articulate or create the structure or form of many pieces, as well as add interest. Treatment of a non-tonic chord as a temporary tonic, for less than a phrase, is considered tonicization.Modulation is the essential part of the art. Without it there is little music, for a piece derives its true beauty not from the large number of fixed modes which it embraces but rather from the subtle fabric of its modulation.

name: Pivot-Chord Modulation
prompt: Find a chord shared by your current key and your target key and use it to slip between them.
definition: Pivot-chord modulation uses a chord common to two keys as the gateway between them.
wikiSummary: In music, modulation is the change from one tonality to another. This may or may not be accompanied by a change in key signature. Modulations articulate or create the structure or form of many pieces, as well as add interest. name: Direct Modulation
prompt: Jump directly from your first drop's key into a new key for the second drop with no preparation.
definition: Direct modulation jumps to a new key without preparation.
wikiSummary: In music, modulation is the change from one tonality to another. This may or may not be accompanied by a change in key signature. Modulations articulate or create the structure or form of many pieces, as well as add interest. name: Truck Driver's Gear Change
prompt: Bump every voicing up a semitone for the final chorus to inject one last burst of energy.
definition: A truck driver's modulation shifts the key up by a semitone or tone for a final section.
wikiSummary: In music, modulation is the change from one tonality to another. This may or may not be accompanied by a change in key signature. Modulations articulate or create the structure or form of many pieces, as well as add interest.
